content.text: Deep research agents perform iterative, multi-source investigation of complex questions by autonomously decomposing goals into sub-queries, executing searches across heterogeneous information sources, extracting and ranking evidence, detecting knowledge gaps, and synthesising findings into structured, cited reports. Unlike single-pass retrieval-augmented generation, deep research agents loop over sources until a confidence threshold or depth limit is reached.

attrs.alt: Deep Research Agent Architecture: query planner → search executor → evidence extractor → gap detector → synthesis engine → final report

content.text: What Is a Deep Research Agent?

A deep research agent is an autonomous system that accepts an open-ended research objective and produces a comprehensive, evidence-backed report by conducting multiple rounds of targeted information retrieval, critical evaluation, and synthesis. The distinguishing characteristic is the iterative loop: after each search round, the agent assesses whether the accumulated evidence is sufficient or whether gaps remain, then plans additional searches to fill those gaps before writing the final report.

The architecture combines a planning LLM (which decomposes questions and identifies gaps), specialised search tools (web search, academic database APIs, document retrieval, code search), an evidence extraction and ranking module, and a synthesis LLM that weaves findings into coherent prose with inline citations. Most production implementations use a single powerful LLM for both planning and synthesis, with the search tools provided through function calling or tool-use APIs.

Deep research agents differ from standard RAG pipelines in three key ways: (1) they actively plan what to search for rather than passively retrieving based on embedding similarity; (2) they detect and address knowledge gaps through iterative search rather than returning whatever chunks are retrieved; and (3) they maintain a growing evidence store that accumulates across multiple search rounds, enabling cross-source synthesis and contradiction detection.

Manual research is time-consuming and cognitively demanding. A research analyst might spend a week searching databases, reading papers, and synthesising findings into a report. A deep research agent can complete an equivalent task in minutes, enabling organisations to scale their research capacity without scaling headcount. This has immediate impact in competitive intelligence, market analysis, scientific literature surveys, legal discovery, and policy research.

Beyond speed, deep research agents can cover more sources than a human researcher in a given timeframe. A human analyst typically reads 20–40 sources per research task; an agent can process hundreds of abstracts, filter for relevance, and extract key claims in the time a human reads a single paper. The breadth advantage is especially significant for cross-disciplinary research where relevant findings may be scattered across multiple domains.

Deep research agents also create an auditable evidence trail. Every claim in the final report is linked to a source document with a passage citation, enabling downstream verification and trust calibration. This is critical in regulated industries where conclusions must be traceable to primary sources.
Massive throughput: processes hundreds of sources per research task vs. tens for a human analyst.
Iterative gap-filling: autonomously identifies missing evidence and plans targeted follow-up searches.
Consistent methodology: applies the same research protocol to every query, eliminating analyst variability and bias.
Auditable citations: every claim links to a specific passage in a specific source document.
Cross-source synthesis: detects contradictions and consensus patterns across diverse sources.
24/7 availability: research tasks run unattended overnight or on a schedule without human supervision.
Breadth across disciplines: searches simultaneously across academic databases, news archives, technical documentation, and web sources.

Deep research agents are costly in both API calls and time. A 10-iteration research loop with 5 searches per iteration at 3 GPT-4o calls per search easily totals 150 LLM calls. At production API rates, this can cost $5–20 per research task, which is acceptable for high-value reports but prohibitive for routine low-stakes queries.

The quality of sources retrieved depends entirely on the quality of the search tools. Web search APIs return top-ranked results that are optimised for click-through rate, not research validity. Academic search APIs (Semantic Scholar, PubMed) cover published literature but not grey literature, industry reports, or informal knowledge bases. Agents that cannot search beyond web results will miss significant evidence in specialised domains.
Hallucination risk in synthesis: even with citations, the LLM may distort source content when synthesising across many passages.
Source credibility blindness: the agent treats a blog post and a peer-reviewed paper as equal evidence unless explicitly instructed to weight by source type.
Circular search: without diversity constraints, the agent may repeatedly retrieve the same small cluster of high-ranking sources.
Context window limits: accumulating evidence across many iterations can fill the context window, forcing truncation of older evidence.
Latency: a 10-iteration loop with sequential searches may take 3–10 minutes even on fast APIs.
Cannot access paywalled content: most academic papers are behind paywalls; the agent works only with abstracts unless full-text access is provisioned.

The research loop begins with a planning step. The LLM receives the research objective and generates a structured research plan: a list of sub-questions that collectively cover the objective, a list of source types to search, and an initial set of search queries. This plan guides the first iteration of the search loop.

In each iteration, the agent executes one search query against each configured search tool, collects the results (titles, abstracts, URLs, or full text), and passes them through an evidence extraction step. The extractor identifies relevant passages, assigns relevance scores, and adds them to the evidence store with source metadata. The planner then reviews the accumulated evidence and identifies gaps: sub-questions not yet answered, claims that need corroboration, or contradictions that require additional sources.

The loop terminates when one of three conditions is met: (1) the gap detector finds no significant remaining questions; (2) the iteration limit is reached; or (3) the accumulated evidence store exceeds a predefined size threshold. At termination, the synthesis engine receives the full evidence store and the original research objective and produces a structured report with section headings, in-text citations, and a bibliography.

Evidence deduplication is critical for quality. Without it, the agent re-reads the same high-ranking passages multiple times, bloating the context window and biasing the synthesis toward frequently cited content. A lightweight embedding-based deduplication step computes cosine similarity between new passages and existing evidence store entries, discarding duplicates above a threshold (typically 0.92).

The diagram above illustrates the complete deep research agent architecture. The Query Planner decomposes the input objective into a structured research plan with sub-queries and source type priorities. The Search Executor runs queries against configured tools in parallel. The Evidence Extractor ranks and deduplicates results, adding them to the evidence store. The Gap Detector evaluates coverage and generates follow-up queries. The Synthesis Engine produces the final report. The dashed re-query arrow shows the iterative loop back from gap detection to search execution.
In-memory dict, Redis, or vector DB
When to Use Deep Research Agents

Deep research agents are appropriate when the research objective is open-ended and requires synthesising evidence from multiple independent sources, when the answer is not known in advance and cannot be retrieved by a single lookup, and when an auditable evidence trail is required for the output.
Competitive intelligence: understanding a competitor's product strategy from press releases, job postings, and patent filings.
Scientific literature surveys: summarising the state of research on a specific hypothesis across dozens of papers.
Due diligence: investigating a company's financial history, legal disputes, and executive background for investment decisions.
Policy research: synthesising evidence from studies, reports, and position papers on a regulatory question.
Technical benchmarking: comparing performance characteristics of alternative technologies across documentation, benchmarks, and user reports.

Deep research agents are NOT appropriate for: simple factual lookups (use a single-turn RAG or search), real-time data requirements (the latency is too high), or tasks where source diversity is irrelevant (use a targeted domain-specific retriever instead).

A minimal deep research agent requires: a planning prompt, search tools, an evidence store, a gap-detection prompt, and a synthesis prompt. The loop connects these components with a controller that manages iterations, deduplication, and termination conditions.

from deep_research_agent import deep_research_agent

# Mock search tool for demonstration
def mock_web_search(query: str) -> list[dict]:
    return [
        {"url": "https://example.com/paper1", "snippet": f"Research on {query}...", "score": 0.8},
        {"url": "https://example.com/paper2", "snippet": f"Study findings for {query}...", "score": 0.75},
    ]

result = deep_research_agent(
    objective="What are the current limitations of large language models in mathematical reasoning?",
    search_tools=[mock_web_search],
    max_iterations=5,
)

print(f"Report ({result['evidence_count']} sources, {result['iterations']} iterations):")
print(result["report"])

A financial services firm uses a deep research agent to generate competitive intelligence reports on emerging fintech companies. The agent receives a company name and produces a 12-page report covering product features, funding history, regulatory filings, key personnel, technology stack signals from job postings, and customer sentiment from review aggregators.

The pipeline runs nightly, updating reports for a watchlist of 200 companies. Each report requires 6–8 iterations covering web search, Crunchbase API, LinkedIn public profiles, regulatory document search, and patent search. Average report generation time is 4.2 minutes; cost per report is approximately $0.85 at GPT-4o mini API rates. Analysts review and annotate reports the next morning, feeding corrections back as ground truth for a fine-tuning pipeline. Report quality improved by 34% over 6 months of this human-in-the-loop feedback cycle.
No deduplication: the agent repeatedly retrieves and re-reads the same top-ranked pages, wasting context and biasing synthesis.
Trusting search rankings uncritically: the first search result is not the most credible; add a source credibility scoring step.
Ignoring contradiction: the synthesis prompt must explicitly instruct the LLM to identify and report contradictions rather than silently averaging them.
Unbounded loops: without a hard iteration cap and a cost ceiling, a stuck agent can run dozens of iterations and bill hundreds of dollars.
Over-generalising from thin evidence: the agent should report confidence levels and evidence counts rather than stating conclusions with false certainty.
Mixing source types without weighting: peer-reviewed papers and anonymous forum posts should not receive equal citation weight.
Set a maximum evidence store size (e.g., 50 items) and trim by relevance score to prevent context overflow.
Use structured output (JSON) for planning and gap-detection steps to make iteration logic deterministic.
Implement source credibility scoring: weight academic papers above 0.8, credible news above 0.6, and forums/blogs below 0.4.
Log all searches and retrieved passages for audit, debugging, and fine-tuning data collection.
Report confidence scores and evidence counts in the final report so downstream users can calibrate trust.
Set hard cost ceilings: abort if cumulative API cost exceeds a threshold and return a partial report.
Deduplicate evidence embeddings with cosine similarity before adding to the store.

Deep research agent latency is dominated by sequential search execution. Running searches serially for 8 iterations with 5 queries each takes 5–15 minutes depending on search API latency. Parallelising searches within each iteration reduces this to 2–5 minutes. Using async Python (asyncio.gather) to execute multiple search tool calls concurrently is the most impactful optimisation.

Token cost scales linearly with evidence store size and iterations. Profile the cost-to-quality tradeoff: in most cases, 4–5 iterations capture 85% of the quality of 10 iterations at 50% the cost. Implement early stopping when the gap detector reports confidence above 0.85 rather than always running to the maximum iteration count.
Returns snippets + raw content; research-focused ranking
Semantic search over web; good for specific claims
Clean content extraction from URLs; handles JS
Tracks every LLM call, token count, and latency

Deep research agents browse external websites and APIs, exposing them to prompt injection attacks embedded in search results. A malicious web page can contain instructions that cause the agent to exfiltrate data, change its research focus, or produce false conclusions. Defend against this by running evidence extraction through a sandboxed summariser that strips formatting and trims content to plain text before passing to the reasoning LLM.

Source authenticity is a reliability concern. The agent cannot verify that a web page accurately represents its claimed author or publication date. Implement source provenance checks: flag results from domains registered less than 6 months ago, require corroboration from a second independent source for any critical claim, and surface a confidence score alongside each conclusion.
Rate limit all search API calls to avoid denial-of-service to external providers.
Validate URLs before fetching: block private IP ranges, localhost, and known malicious domains.
Set timeout limits on all external calls (10–15 s max) to prevent hung loops.
Redact PII from evidence before logging; research tasks may retrieve pages containing personal data.
Implement result content filtering to block NSFW or harmful content from entering the evidence store.
Deep research agents iterate over search-extract-gap-detect cycles rather than making a single retrieval pass, enabling much more comprehensive evidence gathering.
The gap detector is the distinguishing component: it drives iterative search by identifying what evidence is still missing after each round.
Evidence deduplication and source credibility weighting are critical for synthesis quality and preventing context waste.
Parallelise search tool calls within each iteration to cut total latency from 10+ minutes to 2-3 minutes.
Set hard cost and iteration ceilings; an unbounded research loop can be extremely expensive.
The final report should include inline citations, confidence scores, and evidence counts so downstream users can calibrate trust.
Prompt injection via malicious search results is a real threat; sanitise all external content before passing to the reasoning LLM.
